text: The surgeon prepares for laparoscopic intracorporeal bowel anastomosis following the transection of bowel ends during a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon aligns the small bowel and colon after mobilizing the small bowel mesentery. A stay suture is placed on the anti-mesenteric border of both the colon and small bowel, and it is tied down to secure the alignment.

An assistant holds the bowel using a suprapubic port. The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to make a small enterotomy on the small bowel and a corresponding colotomy on the colon, both on the anti-mesenteric border.

The surgeon introduces the stapler, loading the cartridge end into the small bowel. Using the stay suture and graspers, the anvil end is maneuvered into the colon. The surgeon ensures that the lengths of the small bowel and colon limbs are approximately equal.

The surgeon fires the stapler to create a common channel for the ileocolic anastomosis, with no visible evidence of hemorrhage. The first stitch is placed through the staple line, starting on the colon side, passing the needle full thickness through the anastomosis, and then through the small bowel. The knot is tied down on the staple line, and a subsequent stitch replicates this process.
The surgeon uses the left hand to retract and orient the bowel while loading the needle to the corresponding site for suturing. The running stitch is cinched down using the reloaded needle.

The surgeon demonstrates suturing the anastomosis with several throws, inserting the needle full thickness through both sides of the bowel, pulling the stitch through, reloading the needle, and cinching down the stitch to progressively close the anastomosis.

The surgeon places stitches closely together to bring the bowel and colon back together, running the suture up to the stay suture. The stitches are positioned away from the apex of the incision, ensuring robust closure of the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the final few throws of the stitch, finishing the ileocolic anastomosis in one layer. The bowel is carefully oriented to ensure correct alignment of the bowel ends and mesentery, confirming there is no twist, and the result appears satisfactory.
The surgeon completes the ileocolic anastomosis by performing a second layer of sutures, which are either run through the previous layer or imbricated, resulting in a fully constructed anastomosis.
The surgeon extracts the resected colon specimen through a fan-shaped incision, preparing to implement an early recovery strategy for the patient.
